topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay flat on the kitchen counter, stark white against the granite. Liam stared at the results, his thumb tracing the unfamiliar string of letters and symbols. His breath hitched, a shallow, ragged sound in the quiet morning. He reread the section marked 'Biological Parent Match' three times, his eyes blurring slightly. A strange, hollow sensation settled in his chest, heavy and cold. He picked up his phone, his fingers clumsy, and scrolled through old photos, searching for an anchor in the swirling confusion. Every familiar face seemed suddenly distant, overlaid with a layer of impossible complexity. He pushed the paper away, the movement jerky, and walked to the window, watching the street below as if observing a foreign landscape.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." Sarah whispered, the words catching in her throat like shards of glass. She held the test results, the glossy paper feeling impossibly light in her hand. Her hands started to tremble, a faint vibration running up her forearms. She looked down at her own reflection in the darkened glass of the framed picture on the wall—a stranger staring back. A profound stillness overtook her, a complete cessation of movement. She slowly lowered the paper, her gaze fixed on the floorboards, her knuckles white where she gripped the edges of the frame. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on her lungs.

[story 3]

Mark sat rigidly on the edge of the sofa, the DNA report spread across his lap like an accusation. He kept flexing his jaw, a tight, almost painful pressure building behind his teeth. He kept looking at the printed data, his eyes darting back and forth across the genetic markers, trying to find a logical thread in the chaos. A slow, involuntary shake ran through his shoulders, a physical manifestation of the sheer disorientation washing over him. He finally let out a slow, shaky exhale, the sound thin and reedy. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the living room, his steps uneven and quick, unable to settle on any single thought.
